label: Client Apps
description: Netflix apps on Smart TVs, mobile devices, web browsers, and gaming consoles.
role: The user-facing interface that requests content, displays personalized recommendations, and handles video playback via adaptive bitrate streaming.
whyChosen: Platform-specific native apps deliver optimal playback performance and device integration, while the web app leverages React for rapid iteration.

label: Open Connect CDN
description: Netflix's custom Content Delivery Network with appliances embedded in ISP networks worldwide.
role: Caches and delivers video content from the network edge, minimizing latency and backbone bandwidth usage. Handles 100% of video streaming traffic.
whyChosen: Third-party CDNs couldn't deliver the required quality at Netflix's scale. Building their own CDN allowed fine-grained control over caching, routing, and peering relationships.

pros: Closer to end users via ISP partnerships

label: Zuul API Gateway
description: Edge gateway that serves as the front door for all API requests entering the Netflix ecosystem.
role: Handles dynamic routing, authentication, rate limiting, load shedding, and security filtering. Provides a single entry point for all backend microservices.
whyChosen: Netflix needed a highly programmable, async gateway capable of handling millions of concurrent connections with custom filter chains for canary testing and traffic shaping.

pros: Programmable filter pipeline for dynamic behavior
Enables canary deployments and A/B testing at the edge
cons: Complex custom solution requiring deep expertise
Higher operational burden than managed alternatives

label: Eureka Service Discovery
description: Client-side service discovery registry enabling microservices to find and communicate with each other.
role: Maintains a real-time registry of all service instances, their locations, and health status. Services register themselves on startup and query Eureka to find dependencies.
whyChosen: In a cloud environment where instances are ephemeral, hardcoded addresses are impossible. Eureka provides AP (Available, Partition-tolerant) semantics ideal for Netflix's scale.

pros: High availability over strict consistency (AP system)
cons: Requires client-side integration in every service

alternatives.name: Consul
alternatives.context: HashiCorp, supports health checking + KV store

label: API / BFF Services
description: Backend-for-Frontend services that aggregate data from multiple microservices into client-optimized responses.
role: Orchestrate calls to downstream domain services, transform and aggregate data, and return tailored payloads for different client types (TV, mobile, web).
whyChosen: Decoupling client needs from domain service APIs allows independent evolution. Different clients have different data needs that a single API cannot efficiently serve.

pros: Isolates client changes from domain services
cons: Risk of becoming a monolithic aggregation layer

alternatives.name: Direct microservice calls
alternatives.context: Simpler but couples clients to internals

label: EVCache
description: Netflix's distributed caching solution built on Memcached, optimized for high availability and low latency.
role: Provides sub-millisecond access to frequently requested data (user profiles, viewing history, session data). Reduces load on backend databases by orders of magnitude.
whyChosen: Standard Memcached lacked cross-region replication and Netflix-scale operational tooling. EVCache adds automatic failover, zone-aware routing, and data replication.

label: Cassandra
description: Distributed NoSQL database providing linear scalability and high availability with no single point of failure.
role: Primary persistent storage for user data, viewing history, and content metadata. Chosen for workloads requiring massive write throughput and global replication.
whyChosen: Netflix requires a database that scales horizontally across regions with tunable consistency. Cassandra's masterless architecture eliminates single points of failure.

alternatives.name: DynamoDB
alternatives.context: Managed NoSQL with auto-scaling on AWS

label: Apache Kafka
description: Distributed event streaming platform acting as the central nervous system for real-time data pipelines.
role: Enables asynchronous communication between services, powers real-time analytics pipelines, and feeds the recommendation engine with viewing events and user interactions.
whyChosen: Netflix processes hundreds of billions of events daily. Kafka's log-based architecture provides durable, ordered, replayable event streams — essential for both real-time processing and batch analytics.

pros: Event replay capability for debugging and reprocessing
cons: Operational complexity of large Kafka clusters

alternatives.name: RabbitMQ
alternatives.context: Simpler message broker for lower-scale needs

label: Recommendation Engine
description: ML pipeline that personalizes the entire Netflix experience — from homepage rows to artwork selection.
techStack: Custom ML models, TensorFlow, Spark, Flink
role: Analyzes viewing history, ratings, and contextual signals to generate personalized content rankings. Powers the 'Top Picks', genre rows, and even which artwork is shown per title.
whyChosen: Personalization is Netflix's core competitive advantage. 80% of content watched is driven by recommendations. Custom models allow optimization for engagement and satisfaction.

pros: Personalizes beyond just content (artwork, UI layout)
cons: Massive compute costs for training and inference
Cold start problem for new users

label: Titus (Container Platform)
description: Netflix's container management platform running hundreds of thousands of containers on AWS.
role: Manages the lifecycle of all microservice containers — scheduling, scaling, resource isolation, and integration with Netflix's deployment tooling (Spinnaker).
whyChosen: Netflix needed deeper integration with their existing AWS infrastructure and custom scheduling policies that Kubernetes didn't provide at the time of development.

pros: Unified platform for batch and service workloads
cons: Migration cost if moving away from AWS

alternatives.name: AWS ECS
alternatives.context: Managed container service, simpler than K8s
